keyPoints: The talk frames density as a "ladder" of five distinct definitions: visual density (pixels on screen), information density, meaning density, time density, and value density.

Information density draws on Edward Tufte's data-to-ink ratio (goal: as close to 1:1 as possible), exemplified by the 1885 Paris-to-Lyon train schedule that plots stations by physical distance so train speed and transfers are readable from the line angles.

Time density uses HCI thresholds to design feedback: under 100ms feels instant (skip animation), 100ms-10s use transitions/spinners, 10s-1min show a progress bar, and past 1 minute let users leave and notify them later.

The talk's central formula: value density = value users get divided by the time and space you ask them to spend (value per second per pixel), and density isn't fixed, it depends on who is looking (e.g., a chaotic ECG is life-or-death dense to a doctor).

Origin story and conclusion: Stripe CEO Patrick Collison asked why the Stripe dashboard couldn't look like the dense Bloomberg terminal; the answer is that Bloomberg is built for stock brokers while Stripe serves startups and store owners, so density looks different. Ström-Awn argues design's real problem is talking about craft too much rather than doing it.

quotes.text: value density equals the amount of value that your users are getting from what you've designed divided by the amount of time and space that you ask them to spend on it
quotes.talk: Dense by design

quotes.text: I don't think we have a craft problem. I think we have a problem of talking about craft too much.
quotes.talk: Dense by design

talks.title: Building real-time user insights into your design workflow
talks.speakers: Prayag Narula (CEO & Co-Founder, HeyMarvin)
keyPoints: Launched Marvin Live Intercept, billed as the world's first live in-app AI interview intercept: JavaScript embedded in any website/product waits for a behavioral trigger (e.g., user repeatedly clicking the same button, navigating back and forth, or landing on a new app), then prompts a real-time screen-share interview.

The AI interviewer records the user's screen, asks intelligent follow-up questions in 40 different languages, then sends videos back to the Marvin research-analysis account where they can be shared in real time via Slack, Teams, Figma, etc.

Core thesis: scaling/speeding up research (faster surveys, AI moderation) is just 'a faster horse.' The real unsolved problem is capturing user insight in the exact moment before a decision is made, closing the gap between insight and decision-making.

Critiques existing methods: in-app surveys have abysmally low response rates and low fidelity because they force users to translate their mental model into words; after-the-fact interviews force users to reconstruct experiences from memory weeks later. In-the-moment, screen-shared feedback gives completely different fidelity.

Vision framed as 'one researcher per user', letting designers talk to users at the moment of need to match engineering pace without leaving users behind. Origin story: his wife sat on a Zoom call for days waiting for users to click a feedback pop-up, exposing the friction problem.

quotes.text: everyone is pitching it including ourselves how it's a way to get interviewing faster but in the end we're building a faster horse
quotes.talk: Building real-time user insights into your design workflow

quotes.text: The best feedback comes from the moment someone is in your product, not in a survey that they fill out later
quotes.talk: Building real-time user insights into your design workflow

talks.title: Building AI products at enterprise scale
talks.speakers: Dylan Field (CEO & Co-founder, Figma), Jeetu Patel (President and Chief Product Officer, Cisco)
keyPoints: Cisco treats engineering, product management, and design as three equal legs of the stool. Patel deliberately changed the power structure so designers could challenge engineers, and made everyone responsible for design rather than outsourcing it: 'you can't outsource taste,' and as code generation automates, taste becomes the bottleneck.

Design has commercial relevance, and Patel argues design leaders fail when they pitch only idealism instead of money. His three product goals: build products people love (and talk about), reach scaled adoption (the closest proxy to value), and achieve commercial relevance. He credits design as a meaningful contributor to ~$320B of market-cap growth over ~2 years.

Cisco is a full-stack, vertically integrated company (silicon, optics, hardware, OS, security/observability platform, models, apps, agents) and unified 251 acquisitions and 15-20 design languages into one management plane with a single design language for $63B of revenue.

Patel shares his acquisition/product 'tells': he judges companies by typography and whether a product ships in dark mode (default for Cisco) - small nuances reveal whether a team sweats details. He praised Anthropic's distinctive use of serif fonts in a text-based UI.

On AI's future: Patel believes AI creates MORE design jobs, not fewer, because automation makes human judgment, taste, and intuition the profound bottleneck. Tools like Figma Make democratize design to PMs and engineers, which makes designers more valuable, not less. Advice to attendees: coach your boss; if design doesn't matter to your boss, leave.

quotes.text: It takes the same amount of effort to design a bad product than it does to design a good product.
quotes.talk: Building AI products at enterprise scale

talks.title: Managing multiple creative passions in a world that tells you to specialize
talks.speakers: Lauren Hom (Lettering Artist & Designer, Hom Sweet Hom)
keyPoints: Drawing on her 15-year career as a lettering artist and designer, Lauren Hom shares how she has sustained multiple creative passions rather than narrowing to a single discipline.
The talk pushes back against the common industry pressure to specialize, presenting a case for embracing a multi-passion creative path.
Hom frames her experience as guidance for managing and balancing several creative interests over the long term of a career.

talks.title: Usernames on Instagram: how a craft fix became an identity crisis
talks.speakers: Rose McManus (Content Designer, Instagram (Meta))
keyPoints: A craft-level inconsistency (Instagram's story viewer list showing display names instead of usernames, which let spam bots with capitalized/emoji display names float to the top) became the entry point to a much bigger identity question about which name field to standardize on.

McManus uses Baudrillard's four-stage theory of simulation (reality, representation, distortion, masked absence, complete hyperreality) as a framework to analyze usernames vs. display names, e.g., K-pop star IU's handle 'dlwlrma' is her Hangul name typed backwards on a Latin keyboard, a third-stage cipher.

Empirical evidence that users abstract their names: a study of Russian-speaking Instagram users found most abstract their name; on TikTok, 15% of Nigerian users put their real name in their handle while 40% add authenticity markers like 'real' or 'official' (which ironically prove nothing).

Usernames can become hyperreal identities more 'real' than a person's legal name, e.g., the account @dril ('Wint', run by 'some guy named Paul') and creators like Young Kombucha 420 who had to clarify their identity after switching handles.

The product lesson: when designing evolving paradigms, ask what behavior you're enabling beyond your imagination and what happens at 10x/1000x/3 billion-x scale; Instagram ultimately kept usernames because they had already become a load-bearing identity and 'internet export' users built their lives around.

quotes.text: Rose, I need you to press a few buttons while you're at work and get these bots away from me.
quotes.talk: Usernames on Instagram: how a craft fix became an identity crisis

quotes.text: When we We products, we create infrastructure that real people carry with them and ultimately build around.
quotes.talk: Usernames on Instagram: how a craft fix became an identity crisis

talks.title: AI will upend your process, for the better
talks.speakers: Justin Meyer (CEO, Bitovi)
keyPoints: Bitovi sees teams maturing through three agentic phases: solo vibe coders, 'local agent orchestrators' (everyone uses their agent and moves work between sources of truth like GitHub, Jira, Figma), and 'cloud agent orchestrating' teams who trigger agent steps with a button click.

Four prerequisites make non-engineer vibe coding succeed: a mocking layer (Mock Service Worker) so the agent only builds the front end; three core skills (brand, component, data-model skills) for consistency; visual tools like Convey/Figma Make Local so designers drag-and-drop instead of writing technical prompts; and easy sharing via GitHub Codespaces (the Code button > '+') to spin up a shareable environment.

Their React-to-Figma skill layers a visual diff using Storybook on top of Figma's MCP so the agent catches small mismatches (e.g., a checkbox outline thicker in React than Figma) and self-corrects when rebuilding components.
For implementation accuracy, Figma's get_design_context plus Code Connect are essential, Code Connect roughly tripled the likelihood of one-shotting a Figma design in their experience.

Cloud orchestration is implemented cleverly by posting a GitHub issue assigned to GitHub Copilot (or a Bitovi AI agent on a Jira ticket) that runs the exact same skills the team ran locally, enabling a near end-to-end design-to-prod workflow via button clicks; all skills/setup are open source via a QR code.

quotes.text: instead of the traditional weeks-long process of going from design to dev to test and the user testing, product owners, designers, they're building prototypes that take hours
quotes.talk: AI will upend your process, for the better

quotes.text: When code connect in our experience, like I mean, might triple the the likelihood that you're going to one shot a Figma design.
quotes.talk: AI will upend your process, for the better

talks.title: Designing at the pace of model drops
talks.speakers: Rodrigo Lemes (Ux Principal and OPS director, Mercado Livre), Paige Costello (VP Product, Figma)
keyPoints: Mercado Libre has cultivated a culture that encourages risk-taking, making mistakes, and trying again, sustained across a large org of over 1,500 designers within a 30,000-person product, design, and engineering organization spanning many countries, languages, and cultures.
The conversation explores how the company translates its values into everyday practice, keeping a large organization curious, autonomous, and willing to be wrong.
It addresses the rising stakes of AI reshaping design tools almost weekly, and what it takes to design and adapt at that pace.
Framed as a discussion between Mercado Libre Principal Designer Rodrigo Lemes and Figma VP Product Paige Costello.

talks.title: How Codex is rewriting the product playbook
talks.speakers: Alexander Embiricos (Head of Enterprise, OpenAI), Kris Rasmussen (Chief Technology Officer, Figma)
keyPoints: A fireside chat with Alex Embiricos (head of product for Codex at OpenAI) on the vision behind Codex, framed as one of the most influential AI products in the market today.
Covers Codex's evolution from early product decisions through real-world deployment, tracing how the product developed over time.
Explores what Codex reveals about the future of building software and products.
Discusses how AI is transforming team workflows across product, design, and engineering disciplines.

talks.title: Figma learning lab: express yourself with Figma Draw
talks.speakers: Laura Butvinik (Manager, Education Content Strategy, Figma), Rachel Platt (Education Designer, Figma), Kristen Bates (Education Content Strategist, Figma)
keyPoints: A hands-on, collaborative learning lab session where attendees come together to create a single shared art piece.
The session celebrates expressive design and showcases what's possible with Figma Draw.
Attendees are asked to bring their laptop so they can actively participate in the collaborative creation.
Participants are invited to continue afterward in the Learning Lab Lounge for informal time with the Figma team.

talks.title: The future is low-tech: lessons from the early 2000s
talks.speakers: Jésabel DC (Tech Anthropologist & EdTech Founder, The Lifestyle Lab )
keyPoints: Core thesis: technology in the early 2000s wasn't better because designers were more ethical, but because the ad-based business model that rewards 'trapping' users hadn't yet emerged. She proposes 'low-tech' as a middle path between digital over-immersion and total phone abandonment.

Four design lessons drawn from early-2000s artifacts: (1) LEGO instructions, 'if it requires an explanation, it needs a redesign' (embed guidance in the experience, like Super Mario World 1-1 teaching mechanics through play); (2) Loading bars, 'disorientation creates dependency, orientation creates trust,' answering 'Where am I? What's happening? Can I interact? Did my action work?'; (3) Pokemon, 'technology should activate reality, not replace it' (Strava art, Tinder adding in-person events); (4) Animal Crossing, design for anticipation/secure attachment rather than retention/anxious attachment.

Reframes users as a relationship rather than a resource, arguing the extraction model (build something users can't leave, harvest attention, monetize trapped time) will lose to a reciprocity model as regulation and user expectations make user-centered design a competitive advantage.

Speaker's credibility/product: she's a 'tech anthropologist' with 4,000+ hours of fieldwork studying digital lives; co-founder of The Lifestyle Lab, a product ecosystem (a video game, education programs, consulting) whose co-living game mechanic translates into a real-world roommate system that levels up players in real life.

Business model as design constraint: 'our business model decides what our product is free to become.' The Lifestyle Lab avoids monetizing user time by selling to institutions (schools, workplaces, communities) that benefit from users' growth; cites Time Left's subscription/brand-partnership model and Pinterest's 'go offline' campaign as alignment of business and user success.

quotes.text: And we went from designing experiences for our users to designing engagement mechanisms that use their own nervous systems against them.
quotes.talk: The future is low-tech: lessons from the early 2000s

quotes.text: The best thing you can find online is a reason to go offline.
quotes.talk: The future is low-tech: lessons from the early 2000s

talks.title: Inside the Weave workflow
talks.speakers: Gal Sharir (Product Designer, Figma), Ron Baranov (Creative Workflow Advocate, Figma)
keyPoints: Goes behind the scenes of how Weave creative work actually gets made, focusing on the decisions behind the work rather than the polished final frame
Explores the mechanics of an AI-assisted creative workflow including branching, sequencing, iteration, model chaining, and edits
Addresses mistakes and recoveries as part of the process, treating them as a normal part of creating rather than failures to hide
Frames systems thinking as what turns AI from a 'magic trick' into a real, repeatable creative process

talks.title: The hidden craft of interface design in modern film
talks.speakers: Jayse Hansen (Fictional UI Design Director, ØFFGRID)
keyPoints: Jayse Hansen, a fictional UI (FUI) design director, walks through how the holograms, HUDs, and futuristic interface systems seen in blockbuster films are designed, animated, and brought to life, tracing the process from initial sketchbook to the IMAX screen.

The talk emphasizes a story-driven approach to designing technology that doesn't actually exist but feels like it plausibly should, framing fictional interface design as a craft in service of narrative.
Hansen draws on experience spanning more than 20 major franchises, including Iron Man, The Hunger Games, Tron, and Star Wars, offering a rare backstage look at this normally hidden craft.
The session highlights the meticulous, detailed work behind film interface design, going behind the screens to reveal a discipline most audiences never consciously notice.

talks.title: Figma learning lab: workflow tips, from slow to flow
talks.speakers: Chad Bergman (Designer Advocate, Figma), Michel Ferreira (Designer Advocate, Figma)
keyPoints: A pair of Figma Designer Advocates (Chad Bergman and Michel Ferreira) share a collection of practical tips and workflow tricks aimed at helping designers work smarter and faster.
The focus is on speeding up work both on the canvas and beyond it, framing the session around moving from a slow workflow to a state of flow.
The session is positioned as part of Figma's Learning Lab, with an invitation to continue informally afterward in the Learning Lab Lounge for time with the Figma team.

talks.title: Quirk up your workplace
talks.speakers: Darby Thomas (Senior Product Designer, GitHub)
keyPoints: The talk makes the case for bringing more silliness and playfulness into the workplace rather than treating work as strictly serious.
Darby Thomas draws on GitHub's experience to show how playfulness can make remote work more fun and engaging.
It centers on the specific rituals, gimmicks, and stunts the team has used to keep distributed work interesting.

talks.title: The canvas is not dead
talks.speakers: Catt Small (Staff Product Designer, Dropbox)
keyPoints: Be the driver, not the passenger: AI is a powerful force multiplier but you must define the bounds and own the direction. The talk's core test is a contrast between two real cases.

Where AI helped: building an experimental narrative game (inspired by Slack's UI) in a 2-week class deadline. She gave AI the architecture/framework (Yarn, exporting to HTML/JS) so she could spend her cognitive energy hand-writing 20+ distinctly-voiced characters and hand-drawing all 20 avatars.

Where AI subtracted: creating a product vision at Dropbox (700M+ users, ~20 years of product history). AI-generated screens (tried Figma, ChatGPT) lacked nuance and struggled to retain context; reacting to and fixing output became more work than doing it by hand.

Good uses: synthesis of research/data, generating copy variants and placeholder content, prototyping a contained simple flow (e.g. an error or animation state), poking holes in thinking / finding missing use cases, and reframing a message for different audiences (crit vs. director vs. VP).

Avoid AI for: true innovation (it averages; humans' unpredictability is where innovation comes from), human conviction, balancing pros/cons, managing up / persuading leaders, and accountability. Three questions before opening an AI tool: how much context does the problem require, how expansive/divergent must you be, and what is the cost of getting it wrong.

quotes.text: But speed without direction will lead you down a dead end.
quotes.talk: The canvas is not dead

quotes.text: It is unacceptable to say the AI did it as if that's a reasonable answer. It's just not.
quotes.talk: The canvas is not dead

talks.title: Everyone's still eating: building Notion's design system
talks.speakers: Tamara Chu (Design Engineer, Notion), Shauna Sapper (Software Engineer, Notion)
keyPoints: Notion built and migrated a design system in roughly a year (team officially established July 2025) after CEO Ivan tweeted that Notion didn't have one - the speakers liken the migration to whipping a tablecloth off a table while everyone's still eating and laying a new one at the same time.

A design system is framed not as a component library but as 'a set of shared decisions'; foundations came first: a new color system with the same tokens in every palette plus translucent versions of every opaque color (enabling one-command theme swaps and future high-contrast mode), and a fully custom icon set of thousands of icons hand-drawn by one person, Paul Verlu.

AI agents handled mechanical migrations (writing plans in Notion, then executing with type-check/unit-test verification) but struggled with perceptual work like matching old-to-new colors or how an icon sits in its view box - the lesson: make implicit steps explicit and decide per-step whether AI, a teammate, or you should own it.

They used Claude Code + Notion MCP to introspect the codebase and build deprecation trackers (e.g., 529 icons used only once), and a visual-regression tool called Meticulous that records and replays developer activity to surface before/after/diff screenshots, enabling a pipeline where Claude auto-creates PRs and Meticulous checks each one.

Enforcement falls on a spectrum from ignorable (docs, lint warnings) to unavoidable (tests, PR blockers); their favorite is 'ratcheting' - tracking deprecated-pattern usages in a count that can only go down, enforced by a bot named Doug. Agents take the path of least resistance and circumvent rules (one hard-coded a linted-against style prop via a ref), so the same unavoidable guardrails must bind humans and agents alike. End results: every web-app icon replaced, one text/layout/button component each, and all colors migrated to CSS variables over a 56-part run.

quotes.text: We explicitly linted against style props, but AI made it happen anyway. It did it with a ref. Um honestly, it earned our respect.
quotes.talk: Everyone's still eating: building Notion's design system

talks.title: In defense of radical naivete
talks.speakers: Archie Lee Coates IV (Co-Founder, PLAYLAB, INC.), Damien Correll (VP, Brand Studio, Figma)
keyPoints: A fireside conversation between Figma's VP of Brand Design & Creative, Damien Correll, and PlayLab co-founder Archie Lee Coates IV exploring 'radical naivete' as a creative practice: questioning assumptions, challenging conventions, and approaching familiar problems with fresh eyes.
Draws on PlayLab's work across brands, institutions, and cultural experiences to illustrate how crossing disciplines and starting from first principles can produce unexpected breakthroughs.
Frames these ideas as especially relevant for today's leaders, who are navigating new problems and paradigms without established playbooks.

talks.title: How design makes change legible
talks.speakers: Collin Whitehead (VP of Design, Superhuman)
keyPoints: Argues that as teams ship agents, products, and features at unprecedented pace, maintaining clarity for both teams and users becomes harder, and design is uniquely positioned to create coherence across brands, teams, and experiences.
Draws on lessons from scaling Superhuman and integrating new products into the business to illustrate how design can hold things together amid rapid change.
Calls on design leaders to expand their role beyond managing craft toward shaping strategy.

talks.title: In praise of dark mode
talks.speakers: Mehmet Aydın Baytaş (Senior Design Engineer, Attio)
keyPoints: Argues for 'Dark Mode Maximalism', the thesis that all user interfaces should be designed in dark mode, premised on the idea that good design emits as little light as possible.
Reframes dark mode not as a user-selectable toggle but as the default condition of good design itself.
Draws on the speaker's two decades of combined design and engineering practice, as well as 'arcane scholarship,' to support the case.

talks.title: Preparing Rocket for the AI era
talks.speakers: Emily Strobl (Director, Product Design - AI & Design Systems, Rocket), Will Hobick (Design Engineering Lead, Rocket)
keyPoints: Rocket built an end-to-end home ownership platform after acquiring Mr. Cooper (servicing book grew from 2M to 10M homes overnight) and Redfin; the AI platform handles 800,000 chats, 1.8M+ texts, and 160M+ calls per year, and they ship 5x faster than two years ago.

Their foundation is layered: semantic tokens (the name tells you WHY it exists), the Nova design system on top, annotations for intent, and Code Connect so the Figma MCP can read implementation code and verify code matches design.

Live demo walked the pre-approval letter component (in Rocket Assist) end-to-end: PRD to draft, A/B research on blue vs. green variants prototyped and one-click published in Figma Make, research embedded on the Figma canvas, then a design-system drift check that caught detached colors, a contrast failure, and detached dimensions.

Implementation in VS Code used a skill that avoids one-shotting MCP output, it drafts the component, applies annotations, then swaps in design-system utilities; a second Playwright-based skill screenshots Storybook and Figma to compare code and visuals for parity.

Memorable stats: only ~13% of Americans believe home ownership is within reach; 61% of millennial and 65% of Gen Z buyers cried at least once; the connected-workflow experiments drove a $1 billion incremental monthly origination volume increase in Q1 2026 alone.

quotes.text: The hardest part of going AI native isn't getting access to the tools. It's making sure the tools have something solid to build on.
quotes.talk: Preparing Rocket for the AI era

quotes.text: As AI and new tools make it easier to build these new experiences quickly, speed alone is no longer the differentiator.
quotes.talk: Preparing Rocket for the AI era

talks.title: Designing with confidence in an AI-powered world
talks.speakers: Bobby Meixner (VP, Solution Marketing, UserTesting)
keyPoints: UserTesting's report surveyed 183 designers and found a 6:1 speed-vs-confidence gap: 91% say their work moves faster with AI, but only 15% feel much more confident in the quality of their output.

Confidence follows a 'reversibility factor' - it's high in early discovery/ideation (4.1/5) but drops as decisions become irreversible near ship (3.6-3.7); 65% can't confidently say AI makes their outcomes better (37% same, 19% sometimes worse).

The differentiator between designers who feel risk increased vs. decreased is validation: the higher-risk group relies on peer critique or skips validation, while the lower-risk group cross-checks with research, customer feedback, usability tests, and analytics - making defensibility the key variable.

Demoed the UserTesting for Figma plugin plus a Figma Make workflow where 'Diana' analyzes past feedback, launches a test to UserTesting's panel of 7M+ verified participants via AI prompt, gets results back in hours, and resolves a mid-funnel drop-off (completion rate up 79%).

Closing reveal: every 'first-time moment' photo in the deck was AI-generated with deliberate flaws, used to argue that noticing imperfections - human judgment - remains the scarce thing AI can't replace.

quotes.text: one person with the right tools can really build pretty much almost anything. Which sounds amazing until you realize the thing that those 100 people do together
quotes.talk: Designing with confidence in an AI-powered world

quotes.text: to understand people deeply enough to imagine what they don't yet know they need and actually go through and defend that until it truly exists. And that is not something that AI can do for you.
quotes.talk: Designing with confidence in an AI-powered world

talks.title: The next era of product building: Designing the workflows of the future
talks.speakers: Yuhki Yamashita (Chief Product Officer, Figma), Kris Rasmussen (Chief Technology Officer, Figma), Loredana Crisan (Chief Design Officer, Figma)
keyPoints: A fireside-style conversation among Figma's three product leaders, CPO Yuhki Yamashita, CTO Kris Rasmussen, and CDO Loredana Crisan, on how AI is reshaping the way products are imagined, designed, and built.
Examines how teams are adapting their workflows to new ways of working as the software creation process changes alongside AI.
Considers what remains essential and enduring even as the workflows behind product building rapidly evolve.
Looks ahead to the next generation of product creation and the future workflows that will define it.

talks.title: Designed to be read: making a system AI can actually use
talks.speakers: Harvey Whiting (Product Designer, Meta)
keyPoints: AI design tools create a new category Meta calls "fast, but wrong", failing consistently in three ways: low fidelity (wrong type/color, malformed or missing components), low repeatability (same prompt yields different results, eroding trust), and context collapse (a single form works, but a full Ads Manager experience falls off a cliff).

Three root causes for the agent failing: proprietary internal code libraries an open-web-trained model can't bridge, sheer design system complexity (hundreds of components plus do's/don'ts and intentional friction), and unrealistic one-shot expectations for work even experienced designers iterate on.

Meta got scientific using "golden sets" (large batches of test cases run repeatedly with metrics), rebuilt components pixel-exact, and set a goalpost of 80% high accuracy (perfect, production-ready, using every required component), ultimately hitting 87%.

Since manual feedback loops don't scale, Meta co-developed MCP (Model Context Protocol) with Figma to capture every dimension of the design system, color, type, spacing, components, documentation, content standards, illustrations, producing design-system-aligned output in one shot without a template; now broadly available in Figma.

Proposed layered stack for the industry: context (the foundation, anything unspecified gets randomized), then skills (knowing when/why to apply knowledge, launching natively in Figma), then agents. Three takeaways: audit your context, embrace the feedback loop, and prepare for agents.

quotes.text: The question isn't whether AI is part of the workflow, it already is. The question is whether we let it ship slop, or we teach it taste.
quotes.talk: Designed to be read: making a system AI can actually use

quotes.text: Speed without quality is just expensive noise. So, to unlock AI design, we have to solve quality first.
quotes.talk: Designed to be read: making a system AI can actually use

talks.title: Designing brand intelligence: from rules to expression
talks.speakers: Nicole Martinez (Co-founder, Let's Jetty)
keyPoints: Argues that in an AI-powered design world the designer's edge is shifting away from technical execution toward how you think, decide, and design when change outpaces your design system.

Reimagines brand guides as cognitive frameworks rather than rigid rule sets, drawing inspiration from J Dilla, who broke away from rigid quantization in rhythm to create work that felt more human, fluid, and felt.
Offers practical guidance on organizing brands by cognition and building interactive brand frameworks directly in Figma.
Frames an evolution in the designer's role from hands-on makers to strategic directors.

talks.title: Getting real output from Figma Make
talks.speakers: Brett McMillin (Designer Advocate, Figma), Laura Fehre (Designer Advocate, Figma )
keyPoints: Make works with your design system, it just needs to be taught how. The core mechanism is a structured set of markdown guideline files plus a 'make kit' (code representation of components + usage guidelines), which can be built from an NPM package or generated from a Figma library, then published to a private NPM registry inside Figma.

Guideline files should be modular and high-signal, not one generic dump. The recommended structure uses three critical high-level files: a Guidelines file (front door defining product character and required reading order), a Discovery file (teaches the agent where/when/how to find components and verify decisions, preventing hallucinated imports/icons), and a Setup file (exact CSS imports, providers, theme, build config so packages install correctly).

They demoed the full workflow on SAP's Fiori (complex enterprise) and Offtrail (small, no NPM package) design systems: create a kit via the gear/cog 'create a kit' option, select NPM packages (public SAP UI5), drop in generated guideline files, test with a robust screen prompt, then publish. The argument: if it works for SAP's complexity, it works for yours.

Translating human-readable design system guidelines into machine-readable markdown is now trivially fast with an LLM, Laura copied SAP's public guidelines into an LLM with the target structure and it took about 42 minutes; custom 'skills' were written to verify the markdown stayed true and to run a compliance check.

A Fiori compliance-check skill scored generated output 4.7 out of 5 against a 3.8 publish threshold. Beyond kits, they built templates with onboarding overlays and 'presets and recipes' panels (one-click pre-baked prompts to swap floor plans/themes), and noted kit updates to NPM packages and guidelines push down to all consuming template and make files.

quotes.text: Make is great, but it's not using my design system, so it's not that useful for me. We are here today to tell you that's not true.
quotes.talk: Getting real output from Figma Make

quotes.text: for the past 6 months, we've been doing one thing. We've been thinking, talking, and dreaming in markdown.
quotes.talk: Getting real output from Figma Make

talks.title: Digitizing the public sector
talks.speakers: Sau Sheong Chang (DCE (Products) & CTO, GovTech), Marcel Weekes (VP, Product Engineering, Figma)
keyPoints: Sau Sheong Chang draws on his experience building government digital services to explore how the public sector and its institutions can be modernized.
The talk frames modernization as a convergence of three forces: design, technology, and systems thinking working together.
It centers on designing for everyone, treating inclusive, universal design as a core requirement of public-sector digital services.
Chang distills lessons that private-sector leaders can take away from the work of designing services for an entire population.

talks.title: Applied Insights: shifting roles in product building
talks.speakers: Matt Walker (Researcher, Figma), Mike Blight (Manager, Research, Figma)
keyPoints: Figma's research team examines how responsibilities across design, product, and engineering are shifting as roles in product building evolve.
The session explores what these shifting role boundaries mean for how teams collaborate and work together today.
Delivered as an interactive session led by Figma researchers Matt Walker and Mike Blight, drawing on applied insights from their work.

talks.title: Figma learning lab: build your first Weave workflow
talks.speakers: Moran Dankner (Creative workflow advocate, Figma), Ken Vreman (Senior Design Advocate, Figma)
keyPoints: A hands-on, bring-your-laptop workshop that takes attendees from a blank canvas to a working Figma Weave workflow, demystifying node-based tools by actually building with them.
Walks through the core mechanics of Weave: connecting models, directing outputs, and controlling each step of the workflow.
Frames Figma Weave as giving users explicit, granular control over every step of a node-based AI workflow.
Includes informal follow-up time with the Figma team afterward in the Learning Lab Lounge.

talks.title: Own the loop: how designers and researchers win with AI evals
talks.speakers: Setor Zilevu (Researcher, Figma)
keyPoints: Argues that the teams winning with AI succeed not by moving fast but by measuring well, and that good measurement begins with a human-centered definition of what 'good' means, owned by design and research.
Reframes AI evaluation, traditionally treated as an engineering problem, as something that should now be led by designers and researchers.
Claims that as AI capabilities commoditize, design becomes the new competitive moat.
Issues an invitation for designers and researchers to become architects of how AI is evaluated against real human needs.

talks.title: Gnarly by design: building systems with grit
talks.speakers: Beau Wingfield (Visual Product Designer, ex-EA, Full Circle Studio (skate.), Publix)
keyPoints: Beau Wingfield explores how to build gritty, texture-heavy design systems, which he frames as even harder to create than clean design systems.
The talk centers on a real video game design system as a case study for the journey.
It covers techniques for adding character and texture to an otherwise squeaky-clean design system.
A key focus is making such a character-rich, texture-heavy system scalable.

talks.title: 300GB story: how to inspire action when data isn’t enough
talks.speakers: Luke Fiorante (Design Engineer, Harvard University), Sirinda Limsong (Product Designer, Harvard University)
keyPoints: Presented by Luke Fiorante (Design Engineer) and Sirinda Limsong (Product Designer) from Harvard University, the talk centers on a '300GB story' and the challenge of inspiring action when data alone isn't enough.
The session explores how large volumes of data (referenced as 300GB) must be translated into compelling narrative to actually drive people to act.
Figma's official abstract for this Config 2026 session was not yet published at the time of summarizing ('More info coming soon...'), so detailed content specifics are not available.

talks.title: Design context for agentic workflows
talks.speakers: Jake Albaugh (Developer Advocate, Figma)
keyPoints: Warns against "cognitive surrender", adopting an LLM's judgment as your own to avoid the work of validation. Argues AI accelerates execution, not clarity, and that polish (which we're trained to trust as a sign of consideration) is now built on averages and can seduce us off the ideal path. Proposes a "consideration imperative": invest in clarified intent up front.

Frames design as a specification, not just a preview, advocating spec-driven development. Precise context (design tokens, reusable components, Code Connect snippets) packs more intent into less surface area than verbose raw output, likening dumping full visual detail to handing someone encyclopedias when all they need is 'slice of bread, slice of cheese, slice of bread.'

Claims precise context (e.g. Code Connect) makes agents write a far larger share of total code, with less total code overall and lower AI usage, "leaner code and lower AI usage," reducing the tech debt left by verbose context. Cites a Google white paper that low-investment vibe coding yields high maintenance friction and dead-ends for complex systems, vs. up-front platform design.

Introduces "hyper tokens", named, reusable semantic style fragments (extending Figma text styles to shape, layout, typography, effects, motion) compiled from a single strongly-typed, form-agnostic JSON schema (built on the DTCG spec) into libraries, docs, Code Connect, CSS, and Svelte components.

Demonstrates using FigJam as an agentic interface and single source of truth: codifying a Code Connect skill on canvas (with a 'homework' exercise and answer key for agents), letting different agents compile their own skills, and building stegasound.jake.fun, an audio-encoded-image site, in one night across Figma's design agent, Make, and a codebase.

quotes.text: Cognitive surrender is when we adopt an LLM's judgment as our own.
quotes.talk: Design context for agentic workflows

quotes.text: AI accelerates execution, not clarity. If anything, AI can get in the way of clarity.
quotes.talk: Design context for agentic workflows

talks.title: Design for the future of apps
talks.speakers: Craig Labenz (Developer Relations Engineer, Google)
keyPoints: Generative AI extends beyond text chat and image generation to Generative UI (GenUI), a pattern where ephemeral, on-demand UI is composed by AI, increasingly used at Google.
GenUI promises unparalleled personalization, tailoring the interface to each individual user.
This personalization introduces risks of regressions in core UX pillars such as consistency and clear information hierarchy.
The talk shows how designers can adapt design systems to capture GenUI's full potential while mitigating its risks.

talks.title: Designing for agents: rethinking the role of the interface
talks.speakers: Rachel Been (SVP of Design, Expedia), Charlie Sutton (Chief Design Officer, Atlassian), Yuhki Yamashita (Chief Product Officer, Figma)
keyPoints: As AI agents become more common, design is shifting beyond traditional interfaces toward systems that can act on users' behalf, prompting a rethink of the role of the interface itself.
The panel pairs design leaders from both B2B (Atlassian) and consumer (Expedia) contexts, moderated by Figma, to compare how agent-driven experiences play out across different product domains.
Discussion centers on emerging patterns, challenges, and opportunities in designing agent-driven experiences.
A core theme is building agent products that people genuinely understand and feel confident using.

talks.title: The marathon designer: creative endurance beyond prompts and pixels
talks.speakers: Socrates Charisis (Founder & Designer, Quintessential)
keyPoints: Charisis argues that design's biggest threat is not AI but burnout, pixel-pushing, clinging to old ways of working, and waiting for permission.
He introduces a framework for 'creative endurance' built on three core practices: sustainable pacing, builder culture, and versatile hiring.
The talk draws on lessons from running a bootstrapped design studio (Quintessential) in Athens, sharing real stories and actionable systems.
It addresses how to keep creativity alive when the work gets dull and the tools keep changing, emphasizing endurance over speed and chaos.

talks.title: Don't forget the feeling: a critique of software in 2026
talks.speakers: Luis Ouriach (Designer Advocate, Figma)
keyPoints: Framed as a critique of the state of software in 2026, the talk argues that the emotional, human 'feeling' of using software is too often overlooked or lost.
Delivered from the perspective of a Figma Designer Advocate (Luis Ouriach), it centers on how design choices shape the way software feels to use, not just how it functions.
Figma had not released a detailed abstract for this session ahead of Config 2026 (listed only as 'More info coming soon'), so specifics of the argument and examples were not yet published.

talks.title: Designing money: how to build a trustworthy brand in the age of AI
talks.speakers: Chris Warner (Creative Lead, Brand Studio, Plaid)
keyPoints: Explores how to pair machine learning with true artistry in brand and graphic design, and what it means to build a trustworthy financial brand in the age of AI.
Frames the central questions Plaid's team confronted while reshaping how money looks and feels: what's moral, what's safe, and what genuinely resonates in this new creative world.
Examines how authenticity has shaped the history of finance through the lens of graphic design and branding.
Considers what machine learning means for the future of financial design and brand-building.

talks.title: Design systems anarchy
talks.speakers: Lauren LoPrete (Head of Design Foundations, Mercury)
keyPoints: Argues that design systems teams have drifted into a 'cop' or gatekeeper role, creating rules that product teams ignore and route around, rather than enabling good work.
Applies punk's blueprint to design systems: shared ethos, basic infrastructure, and people who care, contending that quality comes from culture rather than compliance.
Draws on the speaker's experience running an indie record label and nearly a decade leading systems at scale at Block, Dropbox, and Expedia to ground the argument.
Claims AI has handed designers the means of production and declared the gatekeeping model dead, calling for teams to stop managing systems and start nurturing subcultures.

talks.title: Legibility by design
talks.speakers: Ryan Powell (Head of Design, Waymo)
keyPoints: Argues that building trust in AI is fundamentally a problem of communication design, not transparency, and that the core challenge across autonomous vehicles and generative agents is the same: deciding how much a system should communicate.
Introduces a framework for trust that moves beyond automation toward 'legibility', designing so users understand a system's intent rather than merely monitoring its tasks.
Emphasizes calibrating feedback to the right level to avoid alert fatigue, drawing on Powell's years of experience designing for Waymo's autonomous vehicles.
Aims to help designers build AI products that feel predictable and fundamentally trustworthy.

talks.title: Closing keynote
talks.speakers: Brent David Freaney (Art Director, Special Offer, Inc.), André Anjos (Artist, RAC)
keyPoints: Brent David Freaney shares the design process behind cultural projects, using the single shade of green developed for Charli XCX's Brat as a focal example of how much craft goes into a single creative decision.
He covers systems thinking as the throughline that holds together large-scale creative campaigns, rather than treating individual deliverables in isolation.
The talk spans design work across multiple mediums: albums, magazines, live media, and screens.
He walks through his actual process and tools, and what it takes to design for cultural impact.
